name: GitHub README and docs
url_or_entry_point: Repository README or official docs for the target project
use_moment: when first deciding whether a project can be deployed
core_promise: show install path and basic operating assumptions
proof_type: stars plus official project status plus search visibility
common_complaints: too idealized and often under-describes production risk or persistence boundaries
why_users_choose_it: fast first stop and close to source of truth
why_users_leave_it: does not always answer public exposure risk maintenance burden or go/no-go judgment
our_possible_response: package scattered install knowledge into a deployment-readiness judgment and ordered checks

name: GitHub issues and discussions
url_or_entry_point: Project issue tracker or discussion board
use_moment: when docs stop being enough or environment-specific bugs appear
core_promise: find someone who hit the same problem and patch together a working path
cta: search threads and ask follow-up questions
common_complaints: answers are fragmented context-dependent and hard to reuse as a checklist
why_users_choose_it: real problems and real edge cases show up there
why_users_leave_it: rarely gives a clean first-5-steps or risk-priority structure
our_possible_response: turn repeated thread pain into reusable article plus checklist plus later project-level judgment

name: ChatGPT or Claude
use_moment: when users want a fast answer before reading long docs
core_promise: quick deployment and troubleshooting advice on demand
proof_type: brand trust plus instant answers plus low friction
common_complaints: advice can be generic lacks project-specific issue density and often avoids strong go-no-go calls
why_users_choose_it: fast and private enough for early framing
why_users_leave_it: hard to trust for high-stakes deployment decisions and sequence planning
our_possible_response: compete on sharper context-specific judgment explicit prioritization and reusable outputs
